title: Watch mode optimization for monorepo with 5000 tests
scenario_context: Monorepo has 5k tests across 12 packages. `vitest --watch` rebuilds everything on each save, taking 8s for what should be a 200ms feedback loop. Engineers stop using watch mode.
what_should_be_tested: That only tests affected by the changed file re-run. Test graph tracks file dependencies; saving src/cart/cart.ts re-runs only cart-related tests, not the whole suite.
anti_patterns_to_avoid: Watch mode running full suite on every save
Disabling watch mode in favor of manual runs
Hardcoded test patterns that miss new files
Restart-on-change of node_modules (slows watch)
test_strategy_reasoning:
Senior thinking:
1. Vitest's built-in dependency graph auto-detects which tests import the changed file. forceRerunTriggers should be empty (let the graph work).
2. Workspace mode in monorepos: configure projects so one package's change doesn't trigger others.
3. Pool: 'threads' vs 'forks' matters for watch — threads share memory, faster restart; forks safer for parallel.
4. UI mode (--ui) gives instant visual feedback without context-switching to terminal.
5. Bug fix loop drops from 8s to 200ms — measurable productivity gain.
mock_strategy: For test-031, Watch mode optimization for monorepo with 5000 tests, mock only nondeterministic clocks, random ids, or external network providers. Keep the real vitest boundary, serializers, auth/session shape, and the assertion target visible: That only tests affected by the changed file re-run. Test graph tracks file dependencies; saving src/cart/cart.ts re-runs only cart-related . Deterministic seed 31 reproduces failures.
edge_cases_tested: Change in one package doesn't trigger others
Newly added test file picked up without restart
UI mode shows live test status
what_would_break_without_this_test: 8s feedback loop kills TDD. With proper config: 200ms for tests touching a changed file. Engineer actually uses watch mode, catches bugs in the editor.
ci_considerations: CI for Watch mode optimization for monorepo with 5000 tests runs with deterministic seed 31, isolated fixture namespace, captured logs/artifacts on failure, max runtime budget tied to vitest, and zero hidden network or wall-clock dependency. Flake policy: one retry only for infrastructure-class failures, then quarantine with owner and failing seed.

title: Testing authentication middleware with all credential paths
scenario_context: Auth middleware accepts: Bearer JWT, session cookie, API key header. Three code paths, one tested. Production: API key path had a typo (`x-api-key` vs `X-Api-Key`) that broke 30% of integration partners.
what_should_be_tested: Every credential type, alone and in conflict: valid JWT, expired JWT, valid cookie, valid API key, no credentials (401), multiple credentials (which wins?). Each path independently verified.
anti_patterns_to_avoid: Testing only the 'common' path
Asserting on JWT internals instead of the auth outcome
Single credential type per test file (you forget the others)
Mocking the auth function so the middleware's case-sensitivity isn't tested
test_strategy_reasoning:
Senior thinking:
1. Auth has multiple credential channels. Each is a separate code path that needs its own test.
2. Header name case-sensitivity: Express normalizes to lowercase, but client SDKs may send any case. Test with weird casing.
3. Conflict resolution rule must be tested: when both JWT and cookie present, which wins? Pick one, document it, test it.
4. The X-Api-Key vs x-api-key bug: middleware checked req.headers['X-Api-Key'] (always undefined in Express since headers are lowercased). Test sending mixed-case headers exposes it.
5. 401 paths: missing creds, malformed creds, expired creds — each distinct.
mock_strategy: For test-032, Testing authentication middleware with all credential paths, mock only nondeterministic clocks, random ids, or external network providers. Keep the real api boundary, serializers, auth/session shape, and the assertion target visible: Every credential type, alone and in conflict: valid JWT, expired JWT, valid cookie, valid API key, no credentials (401), multiple credential. Deterministic seed 32 reproduces failures.
edge_cases_tested: Mixed-case header (regression guard for the actual bug)
what_would_break_without_this_test: The X-Api-Key case bug: `req.headers['X-Api-Key']` is always undefined (Express normalizes to lowercase). 30% of partners broken silently. Test with mixed-case header would have failed immediately.
ci_considerations: CI for Testing authentication middleware with all credential paths runs with deterministic seed 32, isolated fixture namespace, captured logs/artifacts on failure, max runtime budget tied to api, and zero hidden network or wall-clock dependency. Flake policy: one retry only for infrastructure-class failures, then quarantine with owner and failing seed.

title: Page Object Model that scales without becoming a maintenance burden
scenario_context: E2E suite has POMs that grew to 800-line files with 60 methods each. Every test waits on Promise chains because methods are heavily promisified. Tests are slow to read AND slow to run.
what_should_be_tested: Same flows, but with focused, composable POMs. Each POM is small (<150 lines), exposes high-level user intents (login, addToCart), and uses Locator chaining for built-in waiting.
anti_patterns_to_avoid: Mega-POM with every selector and helper
POM methods that return strings (toBe needs to await Locator)
POM with implementation details (clickThenWaitForApiCall)
Locators stored as strings instead of Locator objects (no auto-wait)
test_strategy_reasoning:
Senior thinking:
1. POM = user intents, not selector dump. `loginPage.signInAs(user)` not `loginPage.emailInput.type(...)`.
2. Locators returned (not queried in methods) so tests use Playwright's auto-retry: `expect(loginPage.errorBanner).toBeVisible()`.
3. One POM per page/feature, max ~150 lines. Above that = split.
4. Composition: a `CheckoutFlow` class uses LoginPage, CartPage, PaymentPage. Tests work at the flow level.
5. POMs should NOT do assertions — tests do. POMs do interaction; tests do verification.
mock_strategy: For test-033, Page Object Model that scales without becoming a maintenance burden, mock only nondeterministic clocks, random ids, or external network providers. Keep the real playwright boundary, serializers, auth/session shape, and the assertion target visible: Same flows, but with focused, composable POMs. Each POM is small (<150 lines), exposes high-level user intents (login, addToCart), and uses . Deterministic seed 33 reproduces failures.
edge_cases_tested: Tests own assertions; POMs own interaction
what_would_break_without_this_test: 800-line POMs with embedded asserts: refactoring a button selector breaks 30 unrelated tests. Composed POMs: refactor LoginPage.submit() selector, only login-relevant tests touched. Locator-returning methods give auto-retry; string-storing methods would race.
ci_considerations: CI for Page Object Model that scales without becoming a maintenance burden runs with deterministic seed 33, isolated fixture namespace, captured logs/artifacts on failure, max runtime budget tied to playwright, and zero hidden network or wall-clock dependency. Flake policy: one retry only for infrastructure-class failures, then quarantine with owner and failing seed.

title: Property test for a sort-then-paginate function
scenario_context: paginate(items, page, perPage) sorts by createdAt desc, slices. Production bug: items with identical createdAt appeared on multiple pages OR were skipped entirely, depending on V8's unstable sort behavior.
what_should_be_tested: Invariants: union of all pages equals all input items; intersection of any two pages is empty; page sizes are consistent (last page may be smaller). Across any valid input.
anti_patterns_to_avoid: Examples with all-unique createdAt (misses the bug)
Asserting ordering without asserting completeness
Property that 'pages exist' (passes trivially)
Generators that never produce duplicates
test_strategy_reasoning:
Senior thinking:
1. Stable sort is required when ties exist. Modern V8 has stable sort, but older runtimes don't — and the property should hold regardless.
2. Add a secondary sort key (id) to disambiguate ties. Property test verifies no item appears on 2 pages, none skipped.
3. Generator deliberately includes duplicates in createdAt — that's exactly the bug surface.
4. The 3 invariants: union-equals-input, pairwise-disjoint, size-bounded. All must hold.
5. Shrinker finds minimal repro: 3 items with same createdAt over 2 pages of 2 items each.
mock_strategy: For test-034, Property test for a sort-then-paginate function, mock only nondeterministic clocks, random ids, or external network providers. Keep the real property based boundary, serializers, auth/session shape, and the assertion target visible: Invariants: union of all pages equals all input items; intersection of any two pages is empty; page sizes are consistent (last page may be s. Deterministic seed 34 reproduces failures.
edge_cases_tested: Many items with identical createdAt (forces tie-breaking)
what_would_break_without_this_test: The duplicate/skipped items bug: ties + unstable sort moved items between pages on each call. Disjointness property fails on first run with ~3-item counter-example. Adding id tie-breaker fixes it; property prevents regression.
ci_considerations: CI for Property test for a sort-then-paginate function runs with deterministic seed 34, isolated fixture namespace, captured logs/artifacts on failure, max runtime budget tied to property_based, and zero hidden network or wall-clock dependency. Flake policy: one retry only for infrastructure-class failures, then quarantine with owner and failing seed.

title: Setting up Stryker for a TypeScript Vitest project
scenario_context: Team wants to start mutation testing but Stryker docs assume Jest. First run takes 4 hours and times out on CI; configuration tuning needed before adoption is sustainable.
what_should_be_tested: Setup that makes mutation testing FAST enough to actually run (per-module focus, parallel workers, incremental mode) and reliable enough to gate PRs (timeout tuning).
anti_patterns_to_avoid: Mutating test files (meaningless mutations)
Mutating generated code (slow, no signal)
Single concurrency (4 hours)
No timeout buffer (false survivors from slow tests)
test_strategy_reasoning:
Senior thinking:
1. Mutating all files at once: 5k mutations × 2min suite = 167 hours. Module-scoped: 1 dir → 200 mutations × 20s scoped suite = 67 min.
2. Incremental mode reuses previous mutation report; only mutates changed code on subsequent runs.
3. timeoutFactor 2.0 + timeoutMS 5000: kills truly hanging mutations but allows slow tests.
4. Mutation testing is a periodic audit, not per-PR. Run on main weekly + scoped per-module on demand.
5. Surviving mutants get triaged: kill, document as equivalent, or accept with reason.
solution:
{
  "$schema":"./node_modules/@stryker-mutator/core/schema/stryker-schema.json",
  "packageManager":"pnpm",
  "testRunner":"vitest",
  "testRunnerNodeArgs":["--experimental-vm-modules"],
  "reporters":["html","clear-text","progress","dashboard"],
  "mutate":[
    "src/**/*.ts",
    "!src/**/*.test.ts",
    "!src/**/*.d.ts",
    "!src/generated/**",
    "!src/types/**"
  ],
  "checkers":["typescript"],
  "tsconfigFile":"tsconfig.json",
  "thresholds":{"high":80,"low":60,"break":50},
  "timeoutMS":5000,
  "timeoutFactor":2.0,
  "concurrency":4,
  "incremental":true,
  "incrementalFile":"reports/stryker-incremental.json",
  "dashboard":{
    "reportType":"full",
    "project":"github.com/yourorg/yourrepo",
    "version":"main"
  }
}

// package.json scripts
// "mutation:full": "stryker run",
// "mutation:module": "stryker run --mutate 'src/billing/**/*.ts'",
// "mutation:since-main": "stryker run --incremental"

// .github/workflows/mutation.yml — weekly cron + manual trigger
/*
name: mutation-testing
on:
  schedule: [{cron: '0 4 * * 0'}]
  workflow_dispatch:
jobs:
  mutate:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - uses: actions/setup-node@v4
        with: {node-version: 22}
      - uses: pnpm/action-setup@v3
      - uses: actions/cache@v4
        with:
          path: reports/stryker-incremental.json
          key: stryker-${{ github.ref_name }}
      - run: pnpm install --frozen-lockfile
      - run: pnpm mutation:since-main
      - uses: actions/upload-artifact@v4
        if: always()
        with: {name: stryker-report, path: reports/mutation/}
*/
mock_strategy: For test-035, Setting up Stryker for a TypeScript Vitest project, mock only nondeterministic clocks, random ids, or external network providers. Keep the real mutation boundary, serializers, auth/session shape, and the assertion target visible: Setup that makes mutation testing FAST enough to actually run (per-module focus, parallel workers, incremental mode) and reliable enough to . Deterministic seed 35 reproduces failures.
edge_cases_tested: Timeout factor prevents false survivors from slow tests
what_would_break_without_this_test: 4hr CI run = never runs. Per-module scoping + incremental mode + 4-way parallelism: 12 min weekly run, sustainable. Adoption sticks.
ci_considerations: CI for Setting up Stryker for a TypeScript Vitest project runs with deterministic seed 35, isolated fixture namespace, captured logs/artifacts on failure, max runtime budget tied to mutation, and zero hidden network or wall-clock dependency. Flake policy: one retry only for infrastructure-class failures, then quarantine with owner and failing seed.

title: Snapshot testing visual regressions with Storybook + Chromatic
scenario_context: Component library has 200+ components in Storybook. Designers manually spot-check every release. Subtle visual changes (padding 8→10px, color #fff→#fafafa) slip through, break customer designs downstream.
what_should_be_tested: That every story renders pixel-equivalent to its baseline. Real-browser screenshots at multiple viewports. Diffs reviewed in PR before merge.
anti_patterns_to_avoid: Pixel-perfect threshold on text (font rendering varies)
Capturing animated stories (always different)
One baseline for all browsers (Safari and Chrome differ)
Auto-approving diffs without designer review
test_strategy_reasoning:
Senior thinking:
1. Chromatic (or self-hosted Loki) takes browser screenshots per story. Diffs surface in PR as visual review.
2. Pause animations via Storybook decorator before capture.
3. Per-viewport baselines: mobile + tablet + desktop. Different baselines, different reviews.
4. parameters.chromatic.disableSnapshot for utility stories (logic only, no visual).
5. CI gate: visual changes require approval from a designated reviewer (Chromatic UI Review).
mock_strategy: For test-036, Snapshot testing visual regressions with Storybook + Chromatic, mock only nondeterministic clocks, random ids, or external network providers. Keep the real infrastructure boundary, serializers, auth/session shape, and the assertion target visible: That every story renders pixel-equivalent to its baseline. Real-browser screenshots at multiple viewports. Diffs reviewed in PR before merge. Deterministic seed 36 reproduces failures.
what_would_break_without_this_test: Padding 8→10 ships. Customers' embedded buttons shift. Support tickets. With Chromatic: PR shows diff, designer rejects, change is intentional or reverted.
ci_considerations: CI for Snapshot testing visual regressions with Storybook + Chromatic runs with deterministic seed 36, isolated fixture namespace, captured logs/artifacts on failure, max runtime budget tied to infrastructure, and zero hidden network or wall-clock dependency. Flake policy: one retry only for infrastructure-class failures, then quarantine with owner and failing seed.

title: Testing async iterators / streams without timing flakes
scenario_context: createNewsFeed() returns AsyncGenerator<Post>. Test asserts on collected results, intermittently fails because the generator doesn't yield in the expected order. Real bug masked by 'just rerun the test' culture.
what_should_be_tested: That async iterators yield correct values in correct order with proper cleanup. Test must be deterministic — no race conditions hidden in the test itself.
anti_patterns_to_avoid: Collecting with for-await without break/limit (infinite generators hang tests)
Asserting on order when source has no ordering guarantee
Forgetting to test cleanup (return() / throw())
Mocking time inside the generator without controlling backpressure
test_strategy_reasoning:
Senior thinking:
1. Async iterators have 3 outcomes: yielded value, done, throw. Test each.
2. Take(N) helper: collect N items then close. Prevents test hangs on infinite generators.
3. If order matters, the generator must guarantee it. Don't test for order if upstream is unordered.
4. Cleanup test: explicitly call .return() and verify resources are released (e.g., spy on close handler).
5. Generator that uses setInterval needs FakeClock or vi.useFakeTimers + .advanceTimersByTime + .runAllTicks.
mock_strategy: For test-037, Testing async iterators / streams without timing flakes, mock only nondeterministic clocks, random ids, or external network providers. Keep the real vitest boundary, serializers, auth/session shape, and the assertion target visible: That async iterators yield correct values in correct order with proper cleanup. Test must be deterministic — no race conditions hidden in th. Deterministic seed 37 reproduces failures.
edge_cases_tested: Take(N) prevents hang on infinite generator
what_would_break_without_this_test: Without take()/abort: test hangs forever or runs indefinitely. With proper helpers: deterministic, fast, catches the 'fetch called after abort' leak that wasted DB connections in prod.
ci_considerations: CI for Testing async iterators / streams without timing flakes runs with deterministic seed 37, isolated fixture namespace, captured logs/artifacts on failure, max runtime budget tied to vitest, and zero hidden network or wall-clock dependency. Flake policy: one retry only for infrastructure-class failures, then quarantine with owner and failing seed.

title: Testing components that use Suspense and useTransition
scenario_context: React 19 component uses startTransition to keep UI responsive during a search. Test asserts on loading state but is brittle because the transition's pending state has subtle timing.
what_should_be_tested: That useTransition correctly marks updates as transitions (UI stays responsive), Suspense fallbacks show during pending data, and final UI matches the new query.
anti_patterns_to_avoid: Using waitFor with arbitrary timeouts to 'just wait'
Asserting on internal isPending without observable effect
Mocking startTransition (breaks the very mechanism being tested)
Skipping Suspense fallback assertions
test_strategy_reasoning:
Senior thinking:
1. useTransition's value is that UI stays interactive during the update. Test it by interacting AGAIN during the transition.
2. Suspense fallback shows when a child throws a promise. Test by mocking a deferred-resolving fetcher.
3. React 19's auto-batching means assertions need to await microtasks (await Promise.resolve()) before checking.
4. The right query: by role / by accessible name — same as any component test, regardless of concurrent features.
5. Real bug it catches: a startTransition wrap was missing, search input became unresponsive while results loaded.
mock_strategy: For test-038, Testing components that use Suspense and useTransition, mock only nondeterministic clocks, random ids, or external network providers. Keep the real react component boundary, serializers, auth/session shape, and the assertion target visible: That useTransition correctly marks updates as transitions (UI stays responsive), Suspense fallbacks show during pending data, and final UI m. Deterministic seed 38 reproduces failures.
edge_cases_tested: Loading fallback shows then results appear
Re-typing during pending update doesn't lose keystrokes
what_would_break_without_this_test: Missing startTransition: input freezes while React re-renders the result tree synchronously. Users feel 'laggy typing'. Test catches by typing during the transition and asserting the input value updated.
ci_considerations: CI for Testing components that use Suspense and useTransition runs with deterministic seed 38, isolated fixture namespace, captured logs/artifacts on failure, max runtime budget tied to react_component, and zero hidden network or wall-clock dependency. Flake policy: one retry only for infrastructure-class failures, then quarantine with owner and failing seed.

title: Testing GraphQL resolvers with realistic schema execution
scenario_context: GraphQL API with 80 resolvers. Tests call resolvers directly as functions, bypassing schema execution. Production bug: a missing field in a fragment caused a partial null response, undetected by tests.
what_should_be_tested: Resolvers in context of the schema: querying with realistic GraphQL documents, including fragments, variables, and partial-success scenarios.
anti_patterns_to_avoid: Calling resolvers as plain functions (skips schema, fragments, directives)
Snapshot testing the entire response (brittle)
Mocking the database layer in resolver tests (then it's a unit test, mislabeled)
Testing only successful queries (errors and partial nulls are real responses)
test_strategy_reasoning:
Senior thinking:
1. Use graphql() or executeSync() against the real schema with real resolvers + real test DB. That's the contract clients use.
2. Fragment expansion happens during execution. Test queries should include fragments to catch fragment-specific bugs.
3. Partial errors: GraphQL returns data + errors[]. Test both. Assert that errors[] contains the expected path + message.
4. Variable validation: schema rejects invalid variable types BEFORE the resolver runs. Test that path.
5. Realistic context (auth user, DataLoader) — without it, you skip resolver auth checks.
mock_strategy: For test-039, Testing GraphQL resolvers with realistic schema execution, mock only nondeterministic clocks, random ids, or external network providers. Keep the real api boundary, serializers, auth/session shape, and the assertion target visible: Resolvers in context of the schema: querying with realistic GraphQL documents, including fragments, variables, and partial-success scenarios. Deterministic seed 39 reproduces failures.
edge_cases_tested: Authorization returns null (not throws) for not-found-or-not-yours
what_would_break_without_this_test: Direct resolver invocation: skips schema validation, fragment expansion, directive processing. The partial-null bug shipped because a fragment used a removed field. Schema execution would have errored at parse time.
ci_considerations: CI for Testing GraphQL resolvers with realistic schema execution runs with deterministic seed 39, isolated fixture namespace, captured logs/artifacts on failure, max runtime budget tied to api, and zero hidden network or wall-clock dependency. Flake policy: one retry only for infrastructure-class failures, then quarantine with owner and failing seed.

title: Test database seeding without fixtures shared across tests
scenario_context: Tests share a fixtures file: SEED_USERS, SEED_ORDERS. Test A modifies a user, test B fails because the 'expected' user now has different data. Fixture file grows to 2000 lines, nobody knows what depends on what.
what_should_be_tested: That each test seeds exactly what it needs, in isolation, with no shared mutable state. Per-test seed functions, scoped to the test's transaction.
anti_patterns_to_avoid: Global SEED_DATA imported by 50 tests
Tests that modify shared seeds (breaks the next test)
Truncate-and-reseed before every test (slow)
Seeds with magic IDs ('user-123') tests depend on
test_strategy_reasoning:
Senior thinking:
1. Each test calls seed.* functions that return the IDs created. Test owns the data it touches.
2. Seeds run inside the test's transaction (auto-rollback). Zero cleanup cost.
3. Composable: seedUser → seedOrder(forUser) → seedOrderItems(forOrder). Builds graphs.
4. Returns generated IDs — tests don't hardcode them. Tests don't break when seed IDs change.
5. Factory-backed: seed uses factories (test-008) so data passes the same schema as production.
mock_strategy: For test-040, Test database seeding without fixtures shared across tests, mock only nondeterministic clocks, random ids, or external network providers. Keep the real infrastructure boundary, serializers, auth/session shape, and the assertion target visible: That each test seeds exactly what it needs, in isolation, with no shared mutable state. Per-test seed functions, scoped to the test's transa. Deterministic seed 40 reproduces failures.
edge_cases_tested: Each test fully constructs its data
Returned IDs used (no hardcoded magic IDs)
what_would_break_without_this_test: Shared SEED_USERS: test A changes user 1's email; test B asserts user 1's email is original. Flaky failure. Per-test seeds: tests are independent islands, fail independently, debug-isolated.
ci_considerations: CI for Test database seeding without fixtures shared across tests runs with deterministic seed 40, isolated fixture namespace, captured logs/artifacts on failure, max runtime budget tied to infrastructure, and zero hidden network or wall-clock dependency. Flake policy: one retry only for infrastructure-class failures, then quarantine with owner and failing seed.

title: Test pyramid audit: counting your tests by type
scenario_context: Team has 'tests' but no breakdown. Suspicion: too many slow E2E, not enough fast unit. Need a metric to drive rebalancing.
what_should_be_tested: Distribution of unit/integration/E2E by count AND by time. Target ratio: ~70% unit, ~20% integration, ~10% E2E. Time budget: unit <1s/file, integration <10s/file, E2E <60s/test.
anti_patterns_to_avoid: Treating 'we have lots of tests' as quality signal
Top-heavy pyramid (E2E count > unit count)
Slow tests in tight loops (unit tests >100ms are smelly)
Unmeasured pyramid (you can't improve what you don't track)
test_strategy_reasoning:
Senior thinking:
1. Categorize tests by directory: tests/unit, tests/integration, tests/e2e. Convention enforces structure.
2. Time per test type: unit 50ms median, integration 200ms, E2E 5-10s. Outliers flagged.
3. Coverage by type: unit covers business logic, integration covers boundaries, E2E covers critical user journeys only.
4. Reverse pyramid is the smell — fix by moving E2E coverage down: each E2E that's actually testing business rules should become unit tests.
5. Audit script runs in CI; trends graphed; alerts on drift.
mock_strategy: For test-041, Test pyramid audit: counting your tests by type, mock only nondeterministic clocks, random ids, or external network providers. Keep the real test strategy boundary, serializers, auth/session shape, and the assertion target visible: Distribution of unit/integration/E2E by count AND by time. Target ratio: ~70% unit, ~20% integration, ~10% E2E. Time budget: unit <1s/file, . Deterministic seed 41 reproduces failures.
what_would_break_without_this_test: Team adds 50 E2E in a sprint, no one notices. CI runtime explodes 3 months later. Audit catches drift the week it happens.
ci_considerations: CI for Test pyramid audit: counting your tests by type runs with deterministic seed 41, isolated fixture namespace, captured logs/artifacts on failure, max runtime budget tied to test_strategy, and zero hidden network or wall-clock dependency. Flake policy: one retry only for infrastructure-class failures, then quarantine with owner and failing seed.

title: Snapshot for a complex object using a serializer
scenario_context: Domain object User has nested Address, Profile, lastLoginAt (Date), and uuid. Snapshot tests fail on every run because of changing Date / UUID values.
what_should_be_tested: Stable snapshots of the object's structure and stable fields, with volatile fields normalized to placeholders so diffs reflect real changes.
anti_patterns_to_avoid: Excluding the volatile fields entirely (loses structural validation)
Inline replacement in every test (repetitive)
Date.now mock at test boundary (leaks across tests)
Bespoke serializer per test (couples)
test_strategy_reasoning:
Senior thinking:
1. Vitest's expect.addSnapshotSerializer normalizes the serialization globally.
2. Test: 'is this a Date?' → emit `"<Date>"`. Same for UUIDs. Snapshot becomes stable.
3. Structural diff: change a field name → snapshot diff. Change a Date value → no false diff.
4. Keep the serializer narrow: matches one type at a time. Avoid omnibus that swallows real bugs.
5. Test the serializer itself once — make sure 'looks like a UUID' doesn't false-positive on similar strings.
mock_strategy: For test-042, Snapshot for a complex object using a serializer, mock only nondeterministic clocks, random ids, or external network providers. Keep the real vitest boundary, serializers, auth/session shape, and the assertion target visible: Stable snapshots of the object's structure and stable fields, with volatile fields normalized to placeholders so diffs reflect real changes.. Deterministic seed 42 reproduces failures.
what_would_break_without_this_test: Snapshot diff noise on every run = team disables snapshots. Normalization preserves structural validation while eliminating timing noise.
ci_considerations: CI for Snapshot for a complex object using a serializer runs with deterministic seed 42, isolated fixture namespace, captured logs/artifacts on failure, max runtime budget tied to vitest, and zero hidden network or wall-clock dependency. Flake policy: one retry only for infrastructure-class failures, then quarantine with owner and failing seed.

title: Testing forms with react-hook-form + Zod schema
scenario_context: Profile edit form uses react-hook-form with zodResolver. Test asserts on input values, but doesn't catch that the schema's `bio.max(500)` is not enforced — message shows after 500 chars but submit goes through.
what_should_be_tested: That user input triggers schema validation, errors render correctly, AND submit is blocked while invalid. Test the integration of form library + schema + render.
anti_patterns_to_avoid: Mocking useForm (bypasses real validation)
Asserting on form state instead of submit outcome
Skipping the error message assertion
Testing only field-by-field validation (the form-level rule slips)
test_strategy_reasoning:
Senior thinking:
1. RHF + Zod = real integration. Test by typing user input and clicking submit; verify error visibility and submit-not-called.
2. The submit-handler IS the contract: it should be called with valid data, not at all with invalid data.
3. Multi-field validation (e.g., end-date after start-date) needs form-level test.
4. Async validation (server-side check for unique email) needs await screen.findByText for the error.
5. Resolve mode 'onSubmit' vs 'onBlur' vs 'onChange' is part of the contract; test the configured behavior.
mock_strategy: For test-043, Testing forms with react-hook-form + Zod schema, mock only nondeterministic clocks, random ids, or external network providers. Keep the real react component boundary, serializers, auth/session shape, and the assertion target visible: That user input triggers schema validation, errors render correctly, AND submit is blocked while invalid. Test the integration of form libra. Deterministic seed 43 reproduces failures.
edge_cases_tested: Submit called with exact valid values
Empty optional field (websiteUrl) treated as valid
what_would_break_without_this_test: Schema validates field but submit handler ignored errors. Form 'showed' the error but didn't block submit. Test catches by asserting onSubmit not called when invalid.
ci_considerations: CI for Testing forms with react-hook-form + Zod schema runs with deterministic seed 43, isolated fixture namespace, captured logs/artifacts on failure, max runtime budget tied to react_component, and zero hidden network or wall-clock dependency. title: E2E test for SSE / WebSocket real-time updates
scenario_context: Order status page subscribes via SSE; UI updates when status changes. Existing test just navigates and asserts initial state. The realtime update path is never tested.
what_should_be_tested: That triggering a server-side status change (via API) causes the UI to update without page reload. The full SSE/WebSocket pipeline: connection, message receipt, DOM update.
anti_patterns_to_avoid: Polling the DOM with setTimeout (race conditions)
Mocking the WebSocket (then it's not E2E)
Triggering the update via console.log injection (not a real path)
Asserting only initial state (most of the feature untested)
test_strategy_reasoning:
Senior thinking:
1. Trigger the state change via REAL API call (request.post) so the SSE path fires as in prod.
2. Use Playwright's expect().toHaveText() which auto-retries — handles the small delay between SSE send and DOM update.
3. Verify connection FIRST by checking initial DOM render, then trigger update, then assert new state.
4. Don't assert on the WebSocket frame itself — assert on the user-visible outcome.
5. Mobile/network throttling: SSE reconnect behavior should be tested separately by disconnecting network.
mock_strategy: For test-044, E2E test for SSE / WebSocket real-time updates, mock only nondeterministic clocks, random ids, or external network providers. Keep the real playwright boundary, serializers, auth/session shape, and the assertion target visible: That triggering a server-side status change (via API) causes the UI to update without page reload. The full SSE/WebSocket pipeline: connecti. Deterministic seed 44 reproduces failures.
what_would_break_without_this_test: SSE pipeline silently broke (proxy header stripping); UI showed stale data; nobody noticed for a week. Real E2E catches it on every PR.
ci_considerations: CI for E2E test for SSE / WebSocket real-time updates runs with deterministic seed 44, isolated fixture namespace, captured logs/artifacts on failure, max runtime budget tied to playwright, and zero hidden network or wall-clock dependency. Flake policy: one retry only for infrastructure-class failures, then quarantine with owner and failing seed.

title: Testing pagination cursor semantics
scenario_context: Cursor-based pagination uses base64(createdAt + id). Test fetches page 1 + page 2, asserts no overlap. Bug shipped: when items have identical createdAt, the cursor encoding loses precision, causing duplicates.
what_should_be_tested: That cursor encode → decode → encode is stable, that successive page fetches yield disjoint sets, that the last page returns hasMore=false, and that an invalid cursor returns 400.
anti_patterns_to_avoid: Offset pagination tests (different semantics)
Cursor format opaque to tests (don't test, just trust)
Generating cursor in test instead of server-issued (drifts from real impl)
Skipping the 'identical timestamp' edge case
test_strategy_reasoning:
Senior thinking:
1. Cursor must include all ORDER BY columns to be deterministic. createdAt alone = collisions.
2. Round-trip property: decode(cursor) yields same shape; encode after = same string.
3. Test 'page 1 → cursor → page 2 → cursor → page 3' loop and assert no overlap, no skip.
4. Edge: equal-createdAt items must be ordered consistently by id (secondary sort).
5. Invalid cursor handling: malformed base64, missing fields, out-of-range — each → 400, not 500.
mock_strategy: For test-045, Testing pagination cursor semantics, mock only nondeterministic clocks, random ids, or external network providers. Keep the real api boundary, serializers, auth/session shape, and the assertion target visible: That cursor encode → decode → encode is stable, that successive page fetches yield disjoint sets, that the last page returns hasMore=false, . Deterministic seed 45 reproduces failures.
edge_cases_tested: Identical createdAt resolved by id tie-breaker
what_would_break_without_this_test: Duplicate items on consecutive pages when timestamps collided. Caught by 'all-same-createdAt' test. Without it, ships and customers see same order twice.
ci_considerations: CI for Testing pagination cursor semantics runs with deterministic seed 45, isolated fixture namespace, captured logs/artifacts on failure, max runtime budget tied to api, and zero hidden network or wall-clock dependency. Flake policy: one retry only for infrastructure-class failures, then quarantine with owner and failing seed.

title: Property test for a URL canonicalization function
scenario_context: canonicalUrl(url) normalizes URLs for deduplication (lowercase host, sort query params, strip default ports, etc.). Bug shipped: two URLs that should canonicalize identically don't, causing duplicate analytics events.
what_should_be_tested: Idempotence: canonicalUrl(canonicalUrl(x)) === canonicalUrl(x). Equivalence: URLs that differ only in trivial ways canonicalize to the same string. Across arbitrary URL inputs.
anti_patterns_to_avoid: Properties that test trivially-true things
Using fc.string for URLs (generates garbage)
Skipping idempotence (the most important property of normalizers)
Property tests with no shrinking config (200-line counter-examples)
test_strategy_reasoning:
Senior thinking:
1. Idempotence is the canonical normalizer property. f(f(x)) === f(x). Always test it.
2. Use fc.webUrl() (built-in URL generator) for valid inputs. Avoid garbage strings.
3. Equivalence transformations: append :443 to https, uppercase host, reorder query params — all should yield same canonical.
4. Real bug: query param sorting was case-insensitive on keys, case-sensitive on values. Inconsistent → duplicates.
5. Shrinking returns minimal counter-example URLs — easy to debug.
mock_strategy: For test-046, Property test for a URL canonicalization function, mock only nondeterministic clocks, random ids, or external network providers. Keep the real property based boundary, serializers, auth/session shape, and the assertion target visible: Idempotence: canonicalUrl(canonicalUrl(x)) === canonicalUrl(x). Equivalence: URLs that differ only in trivial ways canonicalize to the same . Deterministic seed 46 reproduces failures.
what_would_break_without_this_test: Analytics duplicates from inconsistent param ordering. Property test catches by generating reordered params and asserting they canonicalize identically.
ci_considerations: CI for Property test for a URL canonicalization function runs with deterministic seed 46, isolated fixture namespace, captured logs/artifacts on failure, max runtime budget tied to property_based, and zero hidden network or wall-clock dependency. Flake policy: one retry only for infrastructure-class failures, then quarantine with owner and failing seed.

title: Documenting equivalent mutants instead of chasing them
scenario_context: Stryker reports 12 surviving mutants. 8 are real (test gaps). 4 are equivalent — semantically identical to original code. Team wastes hours trying to kill the equivalents.
what_should_be_tested: That the 8 real survivors are killed by added tests. The 4 equivalents are documented with `// Stryker disable` comments explaining why they're untestable.
anti_patterns_to_avoid: Adding meaningless assertions just to kill equivalent mutants
Treating Stryker output as gospel without analysis
Disabling Stryker on the file (loses signal on real mutations)
Inflating tests so much that they double-test the same behavior
test_strategy_reasoning:
Senior thinking:
1. Equivalent mutants: cosmetic transforms (e.g., `i++` vs `i+=1`), unreachable branches, redundant defaults. Verify by manual review.
2. Disable per-mutant with clear comment naming the mutator. Reviewer can verify the analysis.
3. Don't disable the whole file — lose detection on future real mutations.
4. 100% kill rate isn't realistic; ~80% with documented equivalents is the bar.
5. Reviewing equivalents teaches the team where the type system already proves invariants.
mock_strategy: For test-047, Documenting equivalent mutants instead of chasing them, mock only nondeterministic clocks, random ids, or external network providers. Keep the real mutation boundary, serializers, auth/session shape, and the assertion target visible: That the 8 real survivors are killed by added tests. The 4 equivalents are documented with `// Stryker disable` comments explaining why they. Deterministic seed 47 reproduces failures.
edge_cases_tested: Empty array boundary (killed the real mutant)
Type-system-prevented equivalents are documented, not chased
what_would_break_without_this_test: Without empty-array test, `lastOrUndefined([])` returns `undefined` either way (>= vs >) — both yield `arr[-1]` which is undefined. Real bug shipped: someone refactors to `arr.length>=2`, breaks single-element case, no test catches. Boundary test prevents both.
ci_considerations: CI for Documenting equivalent mutants instead of chasing them runs with deterministic seed 47, isolated fixture namespace, captured logs/artifacts on failure, max runtime budget tied to mutation, and zero hidden network or wall-clock dependency. Flake policy: one retry only for infrastructure-class failures, then quarantine with owner and failing seed.

title: Hermetic test environments with Testcontainers
scenario_context: Integration tests need Postgres + Redis + Kafka. Devs install locally with inconsistent versions; tests pass on Mac, fail on CI (Linux + Alpine Postgres). Manual setup takes 20 min for new joiners.
what_should_be_tested: Same tests, run anywhere, with infrastructure spun up programmatically using Testcontainers. CI === local === new-joiner experience.
anti_patterns_to_avoid: Per-test container start (slow)
Shared container across worker processes without cleanup
Docker installed but not running (cryptic failures)
Pinning to 'latest' container tags (drift over time)
test_strategy_reasoning:
Senior thinking:
1. Testcontainers boots real services in Docker. Same image as prod = same behavior.
2. Use globalSetup to start containers ONCE per test run, share connection strings via env.
3. Per-worker schema (Postgres) and per-worker namespace (Redis prefix, Kafka topic) for isolation.
4. Pin image tags to exact versions matching production. `postgres:16.4-alpine`, not `postgres:latest`.
5. Reuse mode: in dev, reuse running containers across runs (much faster); in CI, fresh containers per run.
mock_strategy: For test-048, Hermetic test environments with Testcontainers, mock only nondeterministic clocks, random ids, or external network providers. Keep the real infrastructure boundary, serializers, auth/session shape, and the assertion target visible: Same tests, run anywhere, with infrastructure spun up programmatically using Testcontainers. CI === local === new-joiner experience.. Deterministic seed 48 reproduces failures.
what_would_break_without_this_test: 'Works on my machine': local Postgres 15, CI Postgres 16, prod 16. Subtle SQL behavior differs. Testcontainers pins to prod version everywhere.
ci_considerations: CI for Hermetic test environments with Testcontainers runs with deterministic seed 48, isolated fixture namespace, captured logs/artifacts on failure, max runtime budget tied to infrastructure, and zero hidden network or wall-clock dependency. Flake policy: one retry only for infrastructure-class failures, then quarantine with owner and failing seed.

title: Testing a list virtualizer (TanStack Virtual / react-window)
scenario_context: Virtualized list of 10k rows. Test asserts on row content, but only the first ~10 rows are in the DOM at any time. Tests pass even though row 5000 is broken.
what_should_be_tested: That scrolling to a specific row renders it correctly. Virtualization doesn't change WHAT is rendered, just WHEN — tests should still validate the full data flow.
anti_patterns_to_avoid: Asserting `getAllByRole('row')` and expecting 10k (only ~10 in DOM)
Testing only visible rows (5000 may be broken)
Mocking the virtualizer (then it's not testing the virtualized version)
getBy* queries that crash because the row hasn't rendered yet
test_strategy_reasoning:
Senior thinking:
1. Scroll the container to the desired offset, wait for the row to appear, assert.
2. jsdom doesn't compute layout, so getBoundingClientRect returns 0. Mock the scrollable parent's height for the virtualizer.
3. TanStack Virtual exposes `scrollToIndex` — call it imperatively from the test.
4. Use findBy* (waits) not getBy* (immediate); the row appears asynchronously after scroll.
5. Assert on data presence, not on DOM count.
mock_strategy: For test-049, Testing a list virtualizer (TanStack Virtual / react-window), mock only nondeterministic clocks, random ids, or external network providers. Keep the real react component boundary, serializers, auth/session shape, and the assertion target visible: That scrolling to a specific row renders it correctly. Virtualization doesn't change WHAT is rendered, just WHEN — tests should still valida. Deterministic seed 49 reproduces failures.
edge_cases_tested: Only viewport items in DOM (proves virtualization works)
what_would_break_without_this_test: Row 5000 has a rendering bug; test only checks first 10 rows, ships broken. Scroll-to-index test forces rendering of arbitrary rows.
ci_considerations: CI for Testing a list virtualizer (TanStack Virtual / react-window) runs with deterministic seed 49, isolated fixture namespace, captured logs/artifacts on failure, max runtime budget tied to react_component, and zero hidden network or wall-clock dependency. Flake policy: one retry only for infrastructure-class failures, then quarantine with owner and failing seed.

title: Defining 'flaky test' policy and the deletion threshold
scenario_context: Suite has 7 known-flaky tests. They're tagged @flaky and retried 3 times. They've been flaky for 4 months. Whatever bug they reveal is still unfixed; the retry mask is now permanent.
what_should_be_tested: Same behaviors — but with a written policy: flake = bug, not nuisance. Tests have a deadline to fix or delete. Retries are diagnostic, not load-bearing.
anti_patterns_to_avoid: Permanent @flaky tag (becomes the new normal)
Increasing retries to bury flakes
Deleting flaky tests without writing why (lose institutional knowledge)
No policy = silent suite degradation
test_strategy_reasoning:
Senior thinking:
1. Flake = signal of a real race condition or test smell. Suppression hides it.
2. Policy: flake gets a tag + a ticket. 14 days to fix or test gets DELETED.
3. Deletion is fine — flaky tests are often net-negative (false confidence + CI cost + retries).
4. Track flake rate in CI: a dashboard shows per-test flake percentage over time.
5. Convert lessons to durable docs: 'never click without first asserting enabled state' (from real flake).
mock_strategy: For test-050, Defining 'flaky test' policy and the deletion threshold, mock only nondeterministic clocks, random ids, or external network providers. Keep the real test strategy boundary, serializers, auth/session shape, and the assertion target visible: Same behaviors — but with a written policy: flake = bug, not nuisance. Tests have a deadline to fix or delete. Retries are diagnostic, not l. Deterministic seed 50 reproduces failures.
edge_cases_tested: Tests with low run count not yet judged
what_would_break_without_this_test: 7 permanent flakes = 7 unfixed bugs masquerading as 'just CI'. Policy + tracking surfaces them. Forced deletion creates real urgency to fix or accept loss.
ci_considerations: CI for Defining 'flaky test' policy and the deletion threshold runs with deterministic seed 50, isolated fixture namespace, captured logs/artifacts on failure, max runtime budget tied to test_strategy, and zero hidden network or wall-clock dependency. Flake policy: one retry only for infrastructure-class failures, then quarantine with owner and failing seed.

title: Testing module-level singletons that initialize at import
scenario_context: Logger module creates a Pino instance at import: `export const logger = pino(...)`. Test that wants to silence logs can't — module already evaluated when test imports it.
what_should_be_tested: That the logger initialization can be controlled in tests without forking the production module structure. Use lazy init or DI.
anti_patterns_to_avoid: vi.mock the whole module (loses real impl)
Setting env vars after module load (already initialized)
Reading from globals (can't be mocked per test)
Bare singletons evaluated at import time
test_strategy_reasoning:
Senior thinking:
1. Lazy init: `const logger = () => _logger ??= pino(...)`. Test can call a reset before invoking.
2. DI alternative: pass logger to functions. Tests pass test-logger. More verbose but most explicit.
3. vi.mock works but loses real behavior — only use when the module has side effects you can't tolerate.
4. For env-driven config (LOG_LEVEL), set env in test setup before any imports.
5. Pattern transfers to DB clients, telemetry, feature flag SDKs.
mock_strategy: For test-051, Testing module-level singletons that initialize at import, mock only nondeterministic clocks, random ids, or external network providers. Keep the real vitest boundary, serializers, auth/session shape, and the assertion target visible: That the logger initialization can be controlled in tests without forking the production module structure. Use lazy init or DI.. Deterministic seed 51 reproduces failures.
what_would_break_without_this_test: Tests can't silence or capture logs. Suite output noisy; log-based assertions impossible. Lazy + reset pattern enables both.
ci_considerations: CI for Testing module-level singletons that initialize at import runs with deterministic seed 51, isolated fixture namespace, captured logs/artifacts on failure, max runtime budget tied to vitest, and zero hidden network or wall-clock dependency. Flake policy: one retry only for infrastructure-class failures, then quarantine with owner and failing seed.

title: Testing keyboard navigation in a custom menu component
scenario_context: Custom Dropdown component handles arrow keys, Enter, Escape. Bug shipped: pressing arrow down at the last item didn't wrap to first (a11y expectation). Tests asserted click behavior only.
what_should_be_tested: Full keyboard interaction model: arrow up/down navigation, wrap-around, Enter activates focused item, Escape closes menu, Tab moves focus away from menu.
anti_patterns_to_avoid: Only testing click handlers (keyboard is parallel, must be tested)
fireEvent.keyDown without { key } (passes meaningless events)
Asserting on internal focus state instead of document.activeElement
Skipping screen reader expectations (aria-activedescendant)
test_strategy_reasoning:
Senior thinking:
1. Keyboard semantics for a menubar/menu: WAI-ARIA spec. Arrows navigate, Enter activates, Escape closes, Home/End jump.
2. Test with userEvent.keyboard for realistic key events (sequences, modifiers).
3. document.activeElement is the source of truth for focus.
4. aria-activedescendant pattern alternative: the menu has focus, an item has aria-activedescendant — test the attribute changes.
5. The wrap-around bug: arrow down at last item should focus first item. Test it explicitly.

Keep the real react component boundary, serializers, auth/session shape, and the assertion target visible: Full keyboard interaction model: arrow up/down navigation, wrap-around, Enter activates focused item, Escape closes menu, Tab moves focus aw. Deterministic seed 52 reproduces failures.
edge_cases_tested: Home/End not tested here but easily added
what_would_break_without_this_test: Arrow down at last didn't wrap. Keyboard users stuck on last item. Test catches; explicit assertion documents the expected a11y behavior.
ci_considerations: CI for Testing keyboard navigation in a custom menu component runs with deterministic seed 52, isolated fixture namespace, captured logs/artifacts on failure, max runtime budget tied to react_component, and zero hidden network or wall-clock dependency. title: Testing background job processors with BullMQ

what_should_be_tested: That the worker function correctly processes a job, handles failure → retry, and that the queue + worker integration works end-to-end against a real Redis.
anti_patterns_to_avoid: Mocking BullMQ Queue/Worker (then worker logic untested)

5. Cleanup: drain queue + close worker per test.

title: Stateful property test for an LRU cache
scenario_context: LRU cache with capacity limit. After sequence of get/set, the cache should evict least-recently-used. Example tests cover obvious cases; subtle bug: getting an existing key didn't update recency.
what_should_be_tested: That after any sequence of operations: cache size ≤ capacity, get(k) returns the most recently set value or undefined, evictions happen in LRU order.
anti_patterns_to_avoid: Testing only with capacity > N (eviction path untested)
Asserting on internal structure (Map order)
Skipping the 'get updates recency' rule
Property without shrinking config
test_strategy_reasoning:
Senior thinking:
1. State machine: commands are get/set/delete. Model is a Map with manual LRU tracking.
2. After each command on real impl and model, compare visible state: size, contents (via get for each key seen).
3. Capacity bound: real impl never exceeds capacity. Model enforces same.
4. The 'get updates recency' bug: after `set(A); set(B); set(C, capacity=2); get(B); set(D)` — A should be evicted not B if get-updates-recency works.
5. Shrinker finds minimal failing sequence.
mock_strategy: For test-055, Stateful property test for an LRU cache, mock only nondeterministic clocks, random ids, or external network providers. Keep the real property based boundary, serializers, auth/session shape, and the assertion target visible: That after any sequence of operations: cache size ≤ capacity, get(k) returns the most recently set value or undefined, evictions happen in L. Deterministic seed 55 reproduces failures.
edge_cases_tested: Repeated set on same key not double-counted
what_would_break_without_this_test: The 'get doesn't update recency' bug: A B C with cap=2, get(B), set(D) — eviction picks B instead of A. Model agrees with corrected impl; without fix, model and impl disagree → test fails with minimal counter-example.

Flake policy: one retry only for infrastructure-class failures, then quarantine with owner and failing seed.

title: Mutation testing only the diff in a PR
scenario_context: Full mutation run = 10 min. Per-PR full run = unacceptable. But running mutation only on CHANGED files in the PR = ~30s and high signal for new code.
what_should_be_tested: That code added/changed in the PR has tests strong enough to kill mutations. Existing untouched code can keep its current state — the gate is on new code quality.
anti_patterns_to_avoid: Full mutation run on every PR (too slow)
No mutation gate at all (lose signal)
Mutating files in tests/ (meaningless)
Mutation on auto-generated code (noise)
test_strategy_reasoning:
Senior thinking:
1. Stryker's `--mutate` accepts glob patterns. Pass only changed files from `git diff --name-only main`.
2. New code held to higher bar (>= 85% mutation score) than legacy.
3. Caches base run; only mutants in changed code re-evaluated.
4. Combine with line-level: Stryker's diff mode mutates only changed LINES, not whole changed files. Even faster.
5. Output posted as PR comment — author sees surviving mutants inline.
mock_strategy: For test-056, Mutation testing only the diff in a PR, mock only nondeterministic clocks, random ids, or external network providers. Keep the real mutation boundary, serializers, auth/session shape, and the assertion target visible: That code added/changed in the PR has tests strong enough to kill mutations. Existing untouched code can keep its current state — the gate i. Deterministic seed 56 reproduces failures.
edge_cases_tested: Only test changes → no mutation needed
what_would_break_without_this_test: Full Stryker too slow per PR. Without ANY gate, new code lands with weak tests. Diff-mutation gives 80% signal at 5% cost.
ci_considerations: CI for Mutation testing only the diff in a PR runs with deterministic seed 56, isolated fixture namespace, captured logs/artifacts on failure, max runtime budget tied to mutation, and zero hidden network or wall-clock dependency. Flake policy: one retry only for infrastructure-class failures, then quarantine with owner and failing seed.

title: Snapshot serializers for canonical JSON ordering
scenario_context: Tests snapshot API responses. JSON.stringify of an object can produce keys in insertion order; tests are stable on one platform but reorder on another. CI fails on Linux, passes on Mac, for the same code.
what_should_be_tested: That snapshots are stable across platforms. Canonical (sorted) JSON serialization before snapshot.
anti_patterns_to_avoid: Trusting JSON.stringify key order (engine-dependent)
Per-test sorting (repetitive)
Hand-writing sorted snapshots (forgets nested objects)
Snapshot the live response without canonicalization
test_strategy_reasoning:
Senior thinking:
1. Most engines preserve insertion order, but Object spread and Map can produce different orderings.
2. A canonical serializer sorts keys recursively, ensures arrays are sorted by some canonical rule (or left as-is if order is semantic).
3. Apply globally via vitest setupFiles + expect.addSnapshotSerializer.
4. Catches a real class of bugs: API returns sorted differently between code paths; tests catch the inconsistency.
5. For arrays: don't sort by default (order may be semantic); add per-type rules if needed.

Canonical (sorted) JSON serialization before snapshot.. Deterministic seed 57 reproduces failures.

title: Faster tests with in-memory drivers for IO-bound code
scenario_context: File-processing tests write to disk and read back. 5s per test, 200 tests. Suite runtime dominated by disk IO that's incidental to the logic being tested.
what_should_be_tested: Same logic, but with in-memory FS via memfs. Real disk reserved for tests that specifically exercise file system semantics.
anti_patterns_to_avoid: Mocking fs at module level (loses real semantics)
Per-test tmp dirs without cleanup
memfs everywhere (some tests need real semantics like permissions)
Mixing real and in-memory fs without clear boundaries
test_strategy_reasoning:
Senior thinking:
1. Production code reads from injected `fs` interface, not directly. Same code in test gets memfs.
2. memfs is API-compatible with node:fs/promises — drop-in.
3. Tests that NEED real fs (permissions, large files, special filesystems) opt into real fs.
4. 50x speedup on IO-bound suites.
5. Lockstep with prod by occasional 'soak' test against real fs.

Keep the real vitest boundary, serializers, auth/session shape, and the assertion target visible: Same logic, but with in-memory FS via memfs. Real disk reserved for tests that specifically exercise file system semantics.. Deterministic seed 58 reproduces failures.
what_would_break_without_this_test: Slow tests get skipped, real bugs hide. memfs makes them <10ms each, suite runs in CI on every push.
ci_considerations: CI for Faster tests with in-memory drivers for IO-bound code runs with deterministic seed 58, isolated fixture namespace, captured logs/artifacts on failure, max runtime budget tied to vitest, and zero hidden network or wall-clock dependency. Flake policy: one retry only for infrastructure-class failures, then quarantine with owner and failing seed.

title: Testing components that consume Suspense data with React 19 use()
scenario_context: Component uses `use(promise)` to read async data. Tests need to control the promise resolution AND assert on Suspense fallback then final render.
what_should_be_tested: Suspense fallback during pending; final render after resolve; error boundary catches if promise rejects.
anti_patterns_to_avoid: Resolving promise synchronously (skips Suspense path entirely)
No ErrorBoundary in test (rejected promise crashes the test)
Manual setTimeout to 'wait' instead of awaiting findBy
Mocking use() (defeats the purpose)
test_strategy_reasoning:
Senior thinking:
1. use() reads a promise; if pending, suspends. Test must wrap in Suspense and provide a pending promise.
2. To assert fallback, render with an unresolved promise; assert; then resolve and assert final.
3. For rejection, wrap in ErrorBoundary and assert fallback renders the error.
4. Promise must be STABLE across renders (cache it) or use() will keep getting new promises and never resolve.
5. React 19's act() handles the microtask correctly.
mock_strategy: For test-059, Testing components that consume Suspense data with React 19 use(), mock only nondeterministic clocks, random ids, or external network providers. Keep the real react component boundary, serializers, auth/session shape, and the assertion target visible: Suspense fallback during pending; final render after resolve; error boundary catches if promise rejects.. Deterministic seed 59 reproduces failures.
what_would_break_without_this_test: use() bugs (caching, ref stability) don't surface until prod. Promise-control tests give deterministic Suspense behavior.
ci_considerations: CI for Testing components that consume Suspense data with React 19 use() runs with deterministic seed 59, isolated fixture namespace, captured logs/artifacts on failure, max runtime budget tied to react_component, and zero hidden network or wall-clock dependency. Flake policy: one retry only for infrastructure-class failures, then quarantine with owner and failing seed.

title: Smoke tests as production health checks
scenario_context: Production deploys daily. Bug shipped: env var renamed, app started but couldn't connect to DB; users got 503s for 15 min until alerting fired.
what_should_be_tested: Post-deploy smoke: critical endpoints respond, DB connects, dependencies reachable. Runs in CD pipeline against the new deployment URL, BEFORE shifting traffic.
anti_patterns_to_avoid: Smoke tests after traffic shift (too late)
Smoke testing only / (returns 200 even if everything else broken)
No smoke tests (deploys 'pass' even when broken)
Smoke tests that take 10 min (defeats deploy velocity)
test_strategy_reasoning:
Senior thinking:
1. Smoke = small set of HEAD/GET endpoints proving core dependencies work. Run against the new deployment BEFORE LB cutover.
2. /healthz returns 200 only if DB connection, Redis ping, S3 reachable. Smoke calls it.
3. Critical-path checks: GET /api/products returns 200 + non-empty (data-layer alive).
4. Auth check: POST /api/auth/_test (test-only endpoint behind admin token) verifies JWT signing works.
5. <30s total. Block traffic shift on failure. Rollback automatically.
mock_strategy: For test-060, Smoke tests as production health checks, mock only nondeterministic clocks, random ids, or external network providers. Keep the real test strategy boundary, serializers, auth/session shape, and the assertion target visible: Post-deploy smoke: critical endpoints respond, DB connects, dependencies reachable. Runs in CD pipeline against the new deployment URL, BEFO. Deterministic seed 60 reproduces failures.
what_would_break_without_this_test: Env var rename → app starts but DB broken → /healthz returns 503 → smoke fails → traffic NOT shifted → users unaffected.
ci_considerations: CI for Smoke tests as production health checks runs with deterministic seed 60, isolated fixture namespace, captured logs/artifacts on failure, max runtime budget tied to test_strategy, and zero hidden network or wall-clock dependency. Flake policy: one retry only for infrastructure-class failures, then quarantine with owner and failing seed.
